raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Choose the scope of improvement: (A) Light copyedit only (grammar, clarity, concision) with no changes to structure or claims; (B) Copyedit plus structural reorganization and claim–evidence alignment within existing content; or (C) Full substantive edit including reframing the contribution, adjusting/hedging claims, and suggesting citations/related work gaps.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The draft’s core claim and positioning (e.g., SOTA via two modifications) may need reframing for clarity and emphasis, which is central to improvement.",
    "R1.2": "Strengthening the chain from architectural changes to performance conclusions requires checking for logical coherence across premises and conclusions.",
    "R1.3": "Improvement commonly includes ensuring claims (e.g., SOTA, comparisons) align with the reported metrics, datasets, and baselines as written.",
    "R1.5": "Section- or subsection-level organization may need refinement to sharpen the narrative arc beyond sentence-level edits.",
    "R1.6": "Polishing grammar, concision, tone, and readability is a load-bearing part of ‘improve this draft.’",
    "RX.1": "We should disclose uncertainties around baseline comparability, evaluation choices, and the scope of any suggested changes.",
    "RX.2": "Avoiding overstatements (e.g., SOTA or comparisons to production systems) is essential to responsible editing.",
    "RX.3": "Work must stay within improving the draft and not extend into new experiments or unverifiable claims.",
    "RX.4": "Curbing inflated or misleading claims helps prevent downstream misinformation and misuse.",
    "RX.5": "Edits should maintain clear provenance, distinguishing the draft’s reported evidence from editorial judgments."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Choose the scope of improvement: (A) Light copyedit only (grammar, clarity, concision) with no changes to structure or claims; (B) Copyedit plus structural reorganization and claim–evidence alignment within existing content; or (C) Full substantive edit including reframing the contribution, adjusting/hedging claims, and suggesting citations/related work gaps.

rationale.R1.1: The draft’s core claim and positioning (e.g., SOTA via two modifications) may need reframing for clarity and emphasis, which is central to improvement.
rationale.R1.2: Strengthening the chain from architectural changes to performance conclusions requires checking for logical coherence across premises and conclusions.
rationale.R1.3: Improvement commonly includes ensuring claims (e.g., SOTA, comparisons) align with the reported metrics, datasets, and baselines as written.
rationale.R1.5: Section- or subsection-level organization may need refinement to sharpen the narrative arc beyond sentence-level edits.
rationale.R1.6: Polishing grammar, concision, tone, and readability is a load-bearing part of ‘improve this draft.’
rationale.RX.1: We should disclose uncertainties around baseline comparability, evaluation choices, and the scope of any suggested changes.
rationale.RX.2: Avoiding overstatements (e.g., SOTA or comparisons to production systems) is essential to responsible editing.
rationale.RX.3: Work must stay within improving the draft and not extend into new experiments or unverifiable claims.
rationale.RX.4: Curbing inflated or misleading claims helps prevent downstream misinformation and misuse.
rationale.RX.5: Edits should maintain clear provenance, distinguishing the draft’s reported evidence from editorial judgments.
